column2: The Memory capability (#30) focuses on content memory — facts, observations, and task state. But there's a distinct need for user modeling — understanding who the user is, how they communicate, what they prefer, and adapting behavior accordingly. These are fundamentally different: Content memory: "The API endpoint is at /v2/users" → factual recall User modeling: "This user is a senior engineer who prefers terse responses and hates when I explain obvious things" → behavioral adaptation How Hermes solves this Hermes integrates with Honcho, an external user modeling service, as a separate tier from its curated memory: Three tools: 1. honcho_profile : Retrieves a structured peer card: { "name": "Alice", "role": "Staff Engineer", "preferences": ["concise responses", "no emojis", "code-first explanations"], "communication_style": "direct, technical", "expertise": ["distributed systems", "Go", "Kubernetes"], "context": "Currently debugging a race condition in the payment service" } 2. honcho_search : Semantic search over stored context — not just keyword matching, but meaning- based retrieval across all past interactions. 3. honcho_context : Dialectic Q&A — the service synthesizes answers from interaction history using an LLM, answering questions like "What does Alice think about microservices?" by reasoning over past conversations. Integration pattern: Session key mapping: Conversations are mapped to Honcho sessions via build_session_key(platform, user_id, thread_id) Prefetch strategy: Background thread prefetches user context during the previous turn (see Speculative Prefetch for Capabilities (async background data loading) #107) Injection: Context is injected at session-start (first turn) or as turn-specific context (later turns) Non-blocking: If prefetch isn't ready, the turn proceeds without user context Why this is distinct from Memory (#30)
